one_line_takeaway: A statistics-community manifesto arguing that because LLMs are inherently stochastic black boxes, inferential-statistical methods are essential (often the only viable path), and mapping out a 'mosaic' of a dozen open research directions rather than presenting any new method or experiment.
key_finding: The paper's central claim (not an empirical finding) is that statistics is not merely useful but for many LLM problems the only tractable approach, precisely because LLMs are stochastic black boxes; and that the resulting research agenda will be a bottom-up 'mosaic' of problem-specific statistical frameworks rather than one unifying theory. A recurring sub-claim is that scaling laws—where performance keeps improving with model size N without saturation—challenge classical statistical learning theory (which predicts saturation once complexity exceeds intrinsic data dimensionality), which the author flags as an open theoretical puzzle for statisticians.
core_contribution: A single-author position/perspective essay (Weijie Su, stat.ME) that argues, through two connected arguments, that the development and application of LLMs would genuinely benefit from foundational contributions from the statistics discipline—specifically the 'inferential' / 'data-modeling' tradition (Efron's estimation-and-attribution, Breiman's two cultures). Argument 1: LLMs are inherently statistical models because of their strong data dependency (scaling laws, data mixture, 'anything as numeric' embedding) and their stochastic next-token generation, which creates variability and uncertainty that demand statistical treatment. Argument 2: LLMs are persistent black boxes (immense scale, non-unique architectures/optimizers, computational irreducibility), so closed-form mechanistic analysis is intractable and statistical modeling is often the only viable path. The paper's own concrete deliverable is a structured taxonomy (Table 1) of roughly a dozen research directions where statistical methodology is needed—alignment/RLHF, privacy & machine unlearning, fairness, watermarking, speculative sampling, tokenization, uncertainty quantification & calibration, evaluation, hallucination, data mixture & attribution, synthetic data & model collapse, and scaling laws—each paired with its primary objective and candidate statistical methodology. It concludes with the thesis that statistical research on LLMs will form a diverse 'mosaic' of specialized topics rather than a single unifying grand theory, and issues a call for the statistics community to engage now before the field stabilizes.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a non-security perspective paper about the role of the statistics discipline in LLM research. It discusses security-adjacent topics (watermarking, differential privacy, machine unlearning, data attribution) only as examples of research directions, not as attacks or defenses under a threat model. No adversary, asset, or attack surface is defined by the paper's own contribution, so none is imputed here.
limitations: By nature it is an opinion/agenda piece: it presents no experiments, no quantitative evaluation, and no validation of its claims. It is explicitly selective and non-exhaustive, and admits its coverage is narrower than the companion tutorial survey (Ji et al., 2025). It is written from a statistics-discipline vantage point and largely excludes non-GPT/masked models (e.g., BERT) and defers 'how LLMs help statistics' entirely. The mathematical illustrations are cited results, so a reader could mistake surveyed prior work (e.g., Kirchenbauer et al. watermarking, Hoffmann et al. scaling law, Kalai & Vempala's hallucination lower bound) for the paper's own contribution—they are not. It offers no methodology for prioritizing among the dozen directions or measuring impact.
